title: The Coal Comeback: Energy Security Ditches Climate Targets
subhead: The Iran war has exposed the brittleness of the global energy transition. As LNG prices spike 60% and Hormuz remains blocked, Asia and Europe are burning more coal — climate targets are being sacrificed on the altar of energy security, and the winners are the coal exporters who never signed up for the green agenda.

sections.heading: Hormuz Ceasefire on Life Support as Both Sides Dig In
sections.body:
The month-old US-Iran ceasefire is unraveling. President Trump called the truce 'on massive life support' and dismissed Iran's latest peace proposal as 'a piece of garbage' that he hadn't even finished reading. The core disagreements remain unchanged: Washington demands Iran abandon its nuclear program and surrender its 60% enriched uranium stockpile. Tehran insists nuclear talks can only follow the lifting of sanctions and the end of the US naval blockade imposed April 13. The proposal Iran sent via Pakistan on Sunday included demands for US war reparations, full Iranian sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz, an end to sanctions, and release of frozen assets — non-starters from Washington's perspective.

The structural reality is that both sides are calculating they can out-wait the other. Iran believes global oil pain will force American concessions. Washington believes Iranian economic collapse will force regime capitulation. Neither calculation appears correct, and meanwhile the blockade continues. Iran's parliament speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf struck a defiant tone: 'There is no alternative but to accept the rights of the Iranian people as laid out in the 14-point proposal.' Trump heads to Beijing this week to discuss the crisis with President Xi, hoping China can use its leverage with Tehran. But analysts note that China — Iran's biggest economic and strategic partner — is unlikely to resolve Washington's Hormuz dilemma. The longer this stalemate persists, the more structural damage accumulates to global energy architecture.
sections.whyItMatters: A ceasefire that neither side wants to break openly but neither will concede creates a protracted closure scenario. The decisive move for Washington would be a Hormuz military operation to reopen the strait — but that risks broader regional war. For Iran, the trap is believing economic pain will fracture the US coalition before internal Iranian economic collapse triggers regime instability. The third-order effects are already cascading through Asian economies with no end in sight.

sections.heading: Coal Surges as Asia's Energy Security Escape Valve
sections.body:
With LNG prices up more than 60% since the war began and Qatari shipments trapped behind Hormuz, Japan and South Korea — the world's second- and third-largest LNG importers — are turning to coal. The math is brutal: LNG prices in north Asia have spiked over 60% while international seaborne coal prices increased only 13%. In Japan, coal-fired power generation rose 11.1% in April, the biggest increase in a year, while gas power output slumped 13%. In South Korea, the surge was even more pronounced: April coal-fired power supply jumped 40%, the largest increase since August 2019.

This is not a temporary substitution. This is the post-war energy security architecture crystallizing in real time. Countries that thought coal was in terminal decline are realizing that energy security requires redundant supply chains — and coal has them while LNG does not. Global coal imports are on track to reach their third-highest monthly level on record. South Korea has pushed back retirement of coal-fired power capacity. Europe is losing the competition with Asia for spot LNG supply at precisely the moment it needs to fill storage before winter. The climate agenda is being reordered by geography: Asia and Europe will burn whatever keeps the lights on, and the 1.5°C target is becoming a luxury of the pre-Hormuz era.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for coal exporters — Indonesia, Australia, South Africa, Russia — is to lock in long-term contracts while demand is high. For climate policymakers, the trap is assuming this is temporary. Structural energy security concerns are shifting policy responses in ways that will persist well beyond any ceasefire. Wood Mackenzie analysts note that energy security shocks are accelerating coal usage and delaying plant retirements across key Asian and European markets.

sections.heading: OPEC Output Hits 26-Year Low as Iran War Chokes Supply
sections.body:
OPEC oil production has fallen to its lowest level in 26 years as the Iran war takes Persian Gulf barrels offline. The cartel that once commanded the ability to flood or starve global markets is now itself constrained by geography and conflict. Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, UAE, Qatar — the core Gulf producers — are either directly blocked by the Hormuz closure or operating at reduced capacity due to infrastructure damage and export route constraints.

The structural insight is that OPEC's market power has inverted. The cartel's historic leverage came from the ability to withhold supply. Now its members are involuntarily withholding supply due to force majeure, and the leverage has shifted to whatever producers can still access export routes: US shale, Brazil, Russia via non-Hormuz routes, Central Asian crude via pipelines. Morgan Stanley warns that global oil buffers could run out before Hormuz reopens. This is the scenario no strategic planner wanted to model: a prolonged supply shock meeting depleted strategic reserves after the IEA's record 400-million-barrel coordinated release in March.
sections.whyItMatters: The trap for consuming nations is believing strategic reserves buy time for diplomacy. The decisive move would be accelerating alternative supply infrastructure — but that takes years. In the interim, the leverage belongs to any producer with export capacity: US shale, Brazilian offshore, Russian Arctic (sanctions permitting), Central Asian pipelines. OPEC's cartel power has not disappeared but it has transformed from supply management to geography management — who controls which export routes.

sections.heading: Brazil Doubles China Oil Exports as Hormuz Crisis Rewrites Trade Flows
sections.body:
Brazil's oil exports to China have doubled as the Hormuz crisis upends global crude flows. Chinese teapot refiners are slashing output as the Iran war crushes margins, but the larger story is the structural re-routing of supply chains. Brazil — not a Gulf producer — is emerging as a critical alternative source for Chinese crude demand. This is not a temporary expedient but a potentially permanent realignment of trade relationships.

The winners in the Hormuz closure are producers with export routes that bypass the Persian Gulf entirely: Brazil, US shale via Atlantic routes, Russian crude via Pacific routes. The losers are not just Gulf producers but every supply chain that assumed Hormuz would remain open. Japan is receiving its first Central Asian crude cargo this week — Azerbaijani oil via routes that avoid the strait. Japan is also importing Mexican crude starting in July. India's 40+ ships trapped near Hormuz represent a structural vulnerability exposed: over-concentration of supply routes through chokepoints that can close.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Asian importers is diversification of supply routes, not just suppliers. The infrastructure that matters now is pipeline capacity from Central Asia, LNG import terminals served by non-Gulf sources, and shipping routes that avoid chokepoints. The trap is assuming post-war supply chains will look like pre-war supply chains. They won't. The re-routing happening now will persist regardless of when Hormuz reopens.

sections.heading: Qatar Asks LNG Vessels to Go Dark as Iran Targets Gulf Energy
sections.body:
Qatar has asked vessels at its key LNG port to go dark — disabling AIS transponders — for safety as the Iran conflict threatens Gulf energy infrastructure. The request is a tacit acknowledgment that Iran's targeting strategy extends beyond Hormuz to the entire Gulf energy complex. Qatar's Ras Laffan complex, the world's largest LNG facility, sustained damage from Iranian missile strikes in March. The production shutdown and export disruption have removed the single largest LNG supply source from global markets.

The structural insight is that Gulf energy infrastructure — not just shipping lanes — is now contested territory. Qatar going dark is an admission that the world's largest LNG exporter cannot guarantee vessel safety at its own export terminals. This is not a shipping problem; this is an infrastructure survival problem. Every LNG terminal, every oil loading facility, every refinery in the Gulf is now within Iranian missile range. The UAE is running 'ghost tankers' through Hormuz with AIS off to evade Iranian targeting. The entire Gulf export architecture is operating in wartime mode.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for importers is accepting that Gulf LNG and oil exports will carry a permanent risk premium even after any ceasefire. Qatar's multi-year LNG crisis — facilities damaged, customers seeking alternatives — is not a temporary disruption but a structural re-evaluation of Gulf energy reliability. The trap is pricing in a return to 'normal' Gulf exports. Normal is gone.

sections.heading: India Rejects Sanctioned Russian LNG as Secondary Sanctions Bite
sections.body:
India has officially declined Russia's offer to sell LNG from projects under US sanctions, following reports of a Russian LNG-laden tanker unable to discharge after Indian buyers refused delivery. The cargo — approximately 60,000 tons of heavily discounted LNG from Russia's Portovaya plant in the Baltic — is now circling near Singapore with no confirmed destination. Indian buyers and terminal operators refused to accept it after discussions with Russia made clear that violating US sanctions would trigger secondary sanctions risk.

This is a structural shift in India's sanctions compliance posture. India previously maintained Russian oil imports after the Ukraine invasion by emphasizing national energy security and obtaining US waivers. But the Iran war has changed Washington's tolerance for sanctions circumvention. The secondary sanctions threat is now being enforced. India's External Affairs Ministry dismissed reports that it sought a US waiver for Russian LNG, calling them 'speculative.' The message is clear: India will not risk its relationship with Washington over discounted Russian LNG, even as it struggles with the Hormuz supply shock. Energy security is being reordered by sanctions alignment.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Washington is maintaining secondary sanctions pressure while managing the optics of waivers for strategic partners. The trap is believing India's earlier Russian oil imports signaled a permanent sanctions-evasion coalition. India's rejection of Russian LNG shows that secondary sanctions work — when enforced. For Moscow, the calculation is whether discounted energy can find buyers willing to risk US financial system access. Increasingly, the answer is no.

sections.heading: Critical Minerals: US-South Africa Talks Amid China Dominance
sections.body:
Despite tense bilateral relations — Trump accused South Africa of 'white genocide' earlier this year — the US and South Africa held their highest-level meeting so far this year to discuss potential critical mineral deals. The Chinese grip on rare earth elements is driving Washington toward uncomfortable partnerships. China holds 59% of rare earth mining, 91% of refining, and 94% of magnet manufacturing. Since China restricted rare earth exports in early 2025, Western countries have raced to build mine-to-magnet supply chains.

The structural reality is that critical minerals are now contested on the same strategic terrain as oil was in the 1970s. China's export controls revealed that the entire Western defense and EV industries depend on Chinese processing. The US response — Project Vault, the Strategic Critical Minerals Reserve, stakes in mining companies — is a crash program to break the dependency. US-South Africa talks focus on manganese, vanadium, platinum, and chromium — minerals where South Africa has deposits and China has processing dominance. The negotiations are at early stages but point to Washington signing deals across Africa to challenge Chinese control.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for the US is accepting that breaking Chinese critical mineral dominance requires partnerships with governments Washington might prefer not to deal with. South Africa, DR Congo, other African producers — all have governance issues, human rights concerns, or diplomatic tensions with Washington. None of that matters compared to the strategic imperative of reducing Chinese dependency. For China, the trap is overplaying the export control lever. Every restriction accelerates Western alternative supply chain investment.

sections.heading: Türkiye: Energy Inflation Bites as Strategic Position Tightens
sections.body:
While major Asian economies have fiscal buffers to absorb energy price shocks, Türkiye — like other emerging markets — faces a more constrained set of options. The Iran war has eliminated Gulf LNG routes that Türkiye could theoretically access, though the country's primary energy exposure is via oil and gas pipelines from Russia, Azerbaijan, and Iran itself. The closure of Hormuz affects LNG availability globally, pushing up prices for the spot cargoes that Türkiye purchases for peak demand.

Türkiye's strategic position is that it is an energy transit corridor — the Southern Gas Corridor, TurkStream, potential new routes — but not a major energy producer. The current crisis reinforces the value of pipeline diversification: Türkiye can still receive Russian gas via TurkStream and Azerbaijani gas via TANAP, both bypassing Hormuz entirely. However, the broader inflationary impact of global energy prices is hitting Türkiye's economy as it hits India, Vietnam, and other emerging markets. The AP reports that across developing Asia, 'countries with the least resources to respond' feel energy shocks first and hardest. Türkiye is in that category despite its favorable pipeline geography.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye's decisive move is to position itself as the alternative energy corridor for Europe while Hormuz remains blocked. Pipelines through Turkish territory — TurkStream, TANAP, potential future routes — become more valuable with each day the Gulf remains closed. The trap is overestimating how much leverage this provides: Türkiye cannot create new supply, only route existing supply more securely. The economic pain from global energy inflation remains real regardless of favorable transit geography.

bottomLine: The Iran war has exposed a fundamental misalignment between climate policy and energy security architecture. The global energy transition was built on assumptions of stable supply chains and open chokepoints. Neither assumption survived the Hormuz closure. Asia and Europe are burning more coal not because climate targets were abandoned but because the alternative — LNG at 60% price premiums with supply uncertainty — is structurally untenable. The coal comeback is not temporary opportunism; it is a recognition that energy security requires redundant supply chains, and coal has them while LNG does not. OPEC's market power has inverted from supply management to geography management. Washington is discovering that secondary sanctions on Russian energy are enforceable when enforced — India's rejection of sanctioned LNG proves that. The critical minerals race is now operating on the same strategic logic as the 1970s oil security scramble: China has the processing, the West needs the minerals, and the partnerships to break the dependency will be signed with whichever governments have the deposits regardless of diplomatic friction. The ceasefire negotiations are a sideshow to the structural realignment already underway. Even if Hormuz reopens tomorrow, the supply chains being built now — Brazilian crude to China, Central Asian pipelines to Japan, coal plants postponed for retirement — will persist. The decisive insight is that the post-war energy order will not resemble the pre-war energy order, and the winners are whoever controls geography-independent supply routes.
sources: Oilprice.com, Al Jazeera, Reuters, AP News, Financial Times, BIMCO, International Energy Agency, Wood Mackenzie, Bloomberg Intelligence
